title: Opinions & Argumentation
description: The vocabulary of essay-writing and real debate — claim, qualify, refute, persuade.

words.example: Der Minister hat eine Stellungnahme abgegeben.
words.exampleTranslation: The minister issued a statement.
words.whyItMatters: Journalistic register — common in news. 'Eine Stellungnahme abgeben/verfassen' (to issue/draft a statement). Implies a formal, considered position.

words.example: Aus meinem Standpunkt ist das nicht akzeptabel.
words.exampleTranslation: From my standpoint, that's not acceptable.
words.whyItMatters: Formal register. Often paired with 'vertreten': 'einen Standpunkt vertreten' (to hold/represent a position) — classic essay collocation.

words.example: Man muss Vor- und Nachteile abwägen.
words.exampleTranslation: One must weigh up the pros and cons.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich wäge ab'. Perfekt: 'hat abgewogen' (irregular Partizip II). Often pairs with 'Vor- und Nachteile' — typical essay structure word.

words.example: Wir sollten unsere Annahmen hinterfragen.
words.exampleTranslation: We should question our assumptions critically.
words.whyItMatters: NOT separable despite the 'hinter-' prefix. Stronger than just 'fragen' — implies critical examination. Common in academic/journalistic writing.

words.example: Ich zweifle die Echtheit dieses Dokuments an.
words.exampleTranslation: I doubt the authenticity of this document.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich zweifle das an'. Stronger than 'bezweifeln' (which is NOT separable). Pair with 'die Aussage', 'die Echtheit', 'die Behauptung'.

words.example: Sein Argument hat mich nicht überzeugt.
words.exampleTranslation: His argument didn't convince me.
words.whyItMatters: Takes accusative. The adjective 'überzeugt' (convinced) is just as common: 'Ich bin davon überzeugt, dass...'. The noun is 'die Überzeugung'.

words.example: Seine These ist umstritten.
words.exampleTranslation: His thesis is controversial.
words.whyItMatters: Greek-origin, feminine. Common essay phrase: 'eine These aufstellen/verteidigen/widerlegen' (to put forward/defend/refute a thesis).

words.example: Er argumentiert sehr überzeugend.
words.exampleTranslation: He argues very convincingly.
words.whyItMatters: Latin '-ieren' verb — no 'ge-' in Partizip II ('argumentiert'). False friend warning: this is reasoned debate, not fighting (Streit).

words.example: Er behauptet, er sei unschuldig.
words.exampleTranslation: He claims he is innocent.
words.whyItMatters: Often implies the speaker doesn't fully believe what's being claimed. Frequently followed by Konjunktiv I in formal writing (indirect speech).

words.example: Es gibt keinen Beweis für seine Theorie.
words.exampleTranslation: There's no proof for his theory.
words.whyItMatters: Verb 'beweisen' (to prove — strong verb: bewies, bewiesen). 'Ein Beweis für + Akkusativ' (proof of). Plural 'die Beweise'.

words.example: Welche Schlussfolgerung ziehst du daraus?
words.exampleTranslation: What conclusion do you draw from this?
words.whyItMatters: Compound: Schluss + Folgerung. Set phrase: 'eine Schlussfolgerung ziehen' (to draw a conclusion). Academic/essay vocabulary.

words.example: Ich räume ein, dass du recht hast.
words.exampleTranslation: I concede that you're right.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich räume das ein'. Used in argumentation to acknowledge a counterpoint before rebutting it. Perfekt: 'hat eingeräumt'. Mature debate move.

title: Society & Politics
description: The vocabulary of newspapers, political discussions, and engaged citizenship.

words.example: Die Demokratie wird durch freie Wahlen geschützt.
words.exampleTranslation: Democracy is protected by free elections.
words.whyItMatters: Stress on third syllable: 'demo-kra-TIE'. Adjective 'demokratisch'. Compound 'die Demokratiebewegung' (democratic movement) appears often in news.

words.example: Das neue Gesetz stößt auf Widerstand.
words.exampleTranslation: The new law is meeting resistance.
words.whyItMatters: Historically loaded — also refers to Nazi-era resistance ('der Widerstand gegen Hitler'). 'Auf Widerstand stoßen' = to encounter resistance.

words.example: Was ist Ihr Anliegen?
words.exampleTranslation: What's your concern?
words.whyItMatters: Formal register — receptionists and officials will ask this. Different from 'das Problem' (problem) — softer, more constructive. 'Ein wichtiges Anliegen' = an important matter.

words.example: Wir tragen alle Verantwortung für die Umwelt.
words.exampleTranslation: We all bear responsibility for the environment.
words.whyItMatters: Set phrase: 'Verantwortung tragen/übernehmen' (to bear/take on responsibility). The adjective 'verantwortlich' (responsible) takes 'für + Akkusativ'.

words.example: Er engagiert sich ehrenamtlich in der Gemeinde.
words.exampleTranslation: He volunteers in the community.
words.whyItMatters: Specifically unpaid civic work — a major cultural concept in Germany. Noun 'das Ehrenamt' (volunteer work). Adverb usage: 'ehrenamtlich arbeiten'.

words.example: Soziale Gerechtigkeit ist ein wichtiges Anliegen.
words.exampleTranslation: Social justice is an important concern.
words.whyItMatters: Abstract '-keit' nouns are always feminine. Common compound: 'die Steuergerechtigkeit' (tax fairness). The adjective 'gerecht' (just/fair).

words.example: Die Mehrheit der Wähler stimmte dafür.
words.exampleTranslation: The majority of voters voted for it.
words.whyItMatters: Opposite 'die Minderheit' (minority). Often with 'der/die' + genitive: 'die Mehrheit der Bürger'. 'Mit Mehrheit beschließen' = decide by majority.

words.example: Jeder Bürger hat das Recht zu wählen.
words.exampleTranslation: Every citizen has the right to vote.
words.whyItMatters: Female: 'die Bürgerin'. Compounds dominate political language: 'Bürgermeister' (mayor), 'Bürgerinitiative', 'Bürgerrecht'.

words.example: Der Skandal hat die Regierung erschüttert.
words.exampleTranslation: The scandal shook the government.
words.whyItMatters: Sounds international but stress is on the LAST syllable: 'skan-DAL'. Verb 'skandalös' (scandalous). Plural 'die Skandale'.

words.example: Die Regierung hat strenge Maßnahmen ergriffen.
words.exampleTranslation: The government took strict measures.
words.whyItMatters: Constant in news. Set phrase: 'Maßnahmen ergreifen/treffen' (to take measures). Plural 'die Maßnahmen' is almost more common than singular.

words.example: Der Klimawandel hat dramatische Auswirkungen.
words.exampleTranslation: Climate change has dramatic effects.
words.whyItMatters: Almost always plural for impacts. Set phrase: 'Auswirkungen haben auf + Akkusativ' (to have effects on). More formal than 'der Effekt'.

words.example: Wir müssen gegen Vorurteile kämpfen.
words.exampleTranslation: We have to fight against prejudices.
words.whyItMatters: Compound: 'vor' (before) + 'das Urteil' (judgment) — pre-judgment. 'Vorurteile haben gegen' (to have prejudices against). Common in social discussions.

title: Environment & Sustainability
description: The environmental vocabulary that fills German news, classrooms, and policy debates.

words.example: Der Klimawandel ist die größte Herausforderung unserer Zeit.
words.exampleTranslation: Climate change is the biggest challenge of our time.
words.whyItMatters: Compound: 'das Klima' + 'der Wandel' (change). Gender comes from the last part. The single most-used environmental term in German media.

words.example: Wir wollen unseren CO₂-Fußabdruck reduzieren.
words.exampleTranslation: We want to reduce our carbon footprint.
words.whyItMatters: Pronunciation: 'tse-O-zwei'. 'Den Fußabdruck verkleinern/reduzieren' (to reduce one's footprint) is the standard phrase.

words.example: Der Treibhauseffekt verstärkt sich.
words.exampleTranslation: The greenhouse effect is intensifying.
words.whyItMatters: Compound: 'das Treibhaus' (greenhouse) + 'der Effekt'. Related: 'das Treibhausgas' (greenhouse gas). Standard climate-science vocabulary.

words.example: Regenerative Energien werden immer wichtiger.
words.exampleTranslation: Renewable energies are becoming increasingly important.
words.whyItMatters: Synonym: 'erneuerbar'. Both pair with 'die Energien' or 'die Energiequellen'. Latin-origin formal adjective.

words.example: Wir müssen unseren Energieverbrauch reduzieren.
words.exampleTranslation: We have to reduce our energy consumption.
words.whyItMatters: Verb 'verbrauchen' = to use up. Compounds dominate environmental contexts: 'Stromverbrauch', 'Wasserverbrauch', 'Spritverbrauch' (fuel consumption).

words.example: Wir versuchen, nachhaltiger zu leben.
words.exampleTranslation: We're trying to live more sustainably.
words.whyItMatters: Buzzword on product labels everywhere. Noun 'die Nachhaltigkeit' (sustainability) — abstract '-keit' is always feminine.

words.example: Die Erneuerung des Stromnetzes kostet Milliarden.
words.exampleTranslation: Renewing the power grid costs billions.
words.whyItMatters: From 'erneuern' (to renew). Different from 'die Verlängerung' (extension). Common in infrastructure and policy contexts.

words.example: Der Schutz der Umwelt ist unsere Pflicht.
words.exampleTranslation: Protection of the environment is our duty.
words.whyItMatters: Generates endless compounds: 'Umweltschutz', 'Datenschutz', 'Sonnenschutz', 'Tierschutz'. Verb 'schützen' (to protect) + accusative.

words.example: Die Umweltverschmutzung in Großstädten ist alarmierend.
words.exampleTranslation: Environmental pollution in big cities is alarming.
words.whyItMatters: Compound monster but worth knowing — appears constantly in journalism. Smaller compound 'die Luftverschmutzung' (air pollution) is also vital.

words.example: In Deutschland wird der Müll streng getrennt.
words.exampleTranslation: In Germany, trash is strictly separated.
words.whyItMatters: Uncountable — no plural. The strict 'Mülltrennung' system (paper, glass, plastic, organic, residual) is a key cultural feature.

words.example: Naturkatastrophen nehmen weltweit zu.
words.exampleTranslation: Natural disasters are increasing worldwide.
words.whyItMatters: Common news term. Plural 'die Naturkatastrophen'. Often pair with 'zunehmen' (to increase). Related: 'die Überschwemmung' (flood), 'die Dürre' (drought).

words.example: Der Artenschutz ist gesetzlich geregelt.
words.exampleTranslation: Species protection is regulated by law.
words.whyItMatters: Compound: 'die Art' (species) + 'der Schutz' (protection). Related collocation: 'vom Aussterben bedroht' (threatened with extinction).

title: Nuanced Emotions
description: The vocabulary for the complicated, layered feelings adult life actually produces.

words.example: Ich habe ambivalente Gefühle dazu.
words.exampleTranslation: I have mixed feelings about it.
words.whyItMatters: Formal/academic register. International word — easy to remember. Often pairs with 'die Gefühle', 'die Haltung' (attitude), 'die Einstellung'.

words.example: Die Musik klingt melancholisch.
words.exampleTranslation: The music sounds melancholy.
words.whyItMatters: Literary register. Noun 'die Melancholie'. Different from 'traurig' (sad) — implies thoughtful, lingering sadness rather than acute pain.

words.example: Wir alle sind manchmal voreingenommen.
words.exampleTranslation: We're all biased sometimes.
words.whyItMatters: Pairs with 'gegen + Akkusativ' (against) or 'gegenüber + Dativ'. Related noun: 'das Vorurteil' (prejudice). Frequent in social/political discourse.

words.example: Es ist mir gleichgültig.
words.exampleTranslation: It's all the same to me.
words.whyItMatters: Takes dative: 'mir ist es gleichgültig'. Synonym 'egal' is more colloquial. Stronger negative tone than 'egal' — implies emotional disengagement.

words.example: Nach der Erfahrung war ich ernüchtert.
words.exampleTranslation: After the experience I was sobered.
words.whyItMatters: Literally 'un-drunkened' — implies reality crashed in. Stronger than 'enttäuscht' (disappointed) — implies one's idealism was broken.

words.example: Sie war begeistert von dem Vortrag.
words.exampleTranslation: She was enthusiastic about the lecture.
words.whyItMatters: Takes 'von + Dativ'. Stronger than 'froh'. Noun 'die Begeisterung' (enthusiasm). Don't confuse with 'der Geist' alone (spirit/mind).

words.example: Ich war zu Tränen gerührt.
words.exampleTranslation: I was moved to tears.
words.whyItMatters: Past participle of 'rühren' (to stir). 'Zu Tränen gerührt' (moved to tears) is the iconic fixed phrase. Literary register.

words.example: Nach drei Stunden war ich völlig frustriert.
words.exampleTranslation: After three hours I was completely frustrated.
words.whyItMatters: International loanword. Noun 'die Frustration' (formal) or 'der Frust' (colloquial, masculine). 'Frust ablassen' (to vent frustration).

words.example: Mit Wehmut denke ich an die Kindheit zurück.
words.exampleTranslation: I think back on my childhood with wistfulness.
words.whyItMatters: Iconic German emotion-word — beautiful and untranslatable. Adjective 'wehmütig'. Literary/poetic register. Different from 'die Sehnsucht' (longing).

words.example: Er empfindet keine Reue für seine Tat.
words.exampleTranslation: He feels no remorse for his deed.
words.whyItMatters: Stronger and more moral than 'das Bedauern' (regret). Verb 'bereuen' (to regret) + accusative. Common in moral/legal contexts.

words.example: Mit großer Erleichterung hörte ich die Nachricht.
words.exampleTranslation: I heard the news with great relief.
words.whyItMatters: From 'leicht' (light) + 'erleichtern' (to relieve). Adjective 'erleichtert'. Set phrase: 'eine große Erleichterung sein' (to be a great relief).

words.example: Wir waren fassungslos über die Nachricht.
words.exampleTranslation: We were stunned by the news.
words.whyItMatters: Literally 'composure-less'. Stronger than 'überrascht'. Used for genuinely shocking news. Takes 'über + Akkusativ' for source.

title: Technology & Digital Life
description: The vocabulary of contemporary tech, AI, and digital citizenship — read tech news in German.

words.example: Der Algorithmus entscheidet, was du siehst.
words.exampleTranslation: The algorithm decides what you see.
words.whyItMatters: Greek origin, masculine. Plural is irregular: 'die Algorithmen'. Stress on third syllable: 'al-go-RITH-mus'. Frequent in news about social media.

words.example: Datenschutz wird in Deutschland sehr ernst genommen.
words.exampleTranslation: Data protection is taken very seriously in Germany.
words.whyItMatters: Major cultural concept — Germany has some of the strictest privacy laws in the world (DSGVO/GDPR). The word appears in every cookie banner.

words.example: In der App wurde eine Sicherheitslücke entdeckt.
words.exampleTranslation: A security vulnerability was discovered in the app.
words.whyItMatters: Compound: 'die Sicherheit' (security) + 'die Lücke' (gap). Tech-journalism vocabulary. 'Eine Sicherheitslücke schließen' (to close a vulnerability).

words.example: Ich speichere alles im Cloud-Speicher.
words.exampleTranslation: I save everything in cloud storage.
words.whyItMatters: English 'cloud' + German 'Speicher' — typical hybrid. Also 'die Cloud' is widely used alone. Verb 'speichern' (to save/store).

words.example: Die künstliche Intelligenz verändert die Arbeitswelt.
words.exampleTranslation: Artificial intelligence is changing the world of work.
words.whyItMatters: Abbreviated 'KI' in German (NOT 'AI'). 'Eine KI nutzen' = to use an AI. Currently the most-discussed tech topic in German media.

words.example: Welche Suchmaschine benutzt du am häufigsten?
words.exampleTranslation: Which search engine do you use most often?
words.whyItMatters: Compound: 'suchen' (search) + 'die Maschine'. The verb 'googeln' is officially in the Duden dictionary as a German verb.

words.example: Diese Datei lässt sich nicht öffnen.
words.exampleTranslation: This file won't open.
words.whyItMatters: Specifically digital files (not paper — that's 'die Akte'). Plural 'die Dateien'. Common verbs: 'speichern' (save), 'herunterladen' (download), 'löschen' (delete).

words.example: Diese Nachrichten sind Ende-zu-Ende verschlüsselt.
words.exampleTranslation: These messages are end-to-end encrypted.
words.whyItMatters: From 'der Schlüssel' (key). Regular verb. Noun 'die Verschlüsselung'. Important in privacy/security contexts.

words.example: Diese Anwendung läuft nur unter Windows.
words.exampleTranslation: This application only runs on Windows.
words.whyItMatters: German alternative to 'die App' (English loanword, also widely used). Has a non-tech meaning too: 'die Anwendung des Gesetzes' (the application of the law).

words.example: Der Benutzer muss sein Passwort regelmäßig ändern.
words.exampleTranslation: The user must change their password regularly.
words.whyItMatters: Female 'die Benutzerin'. Synonym 'der Nutzer' is equally common. Verb 'benutzen' / 'nutzen' (to use). 'Benutzerkonto' (user account).

words.example: Bitte aktualisieren Sie Ihre App.
words.exampleTranslation: Please update your app.
words.whyItMatters: Latin '-ieren' verb — no 'ge-' in Partizip II ('aktualisiert'). Noun 'die Aktualisierung' or English 'das Update' — both used.

words.example: Die Benutzerschnittstelle ist sehr intuitiv.
words.exampleTranslation: The user interface is very intuitive.
words.whyItMatters: Compound: 'der Schnitt' (cut) + 'die Stelle' (spot/place). 'Die Benutzerschnittstelle' (user interface). Also used for APIs in programming contexts.

title: Sophisticated Adjectives
description: Abstract, formal, and journalistic adjectives that mark you as a confident B1+ speaker.

words.example: Sein Urteil ist voreingenommen.
words.exampleTranslation: His judgment is biased.
words.whyItMatters: Often pairs with 'das Urteil', 'die Meinung', 'die Berichterstattung' (reporting). Takes 'gegen + Akkusativ' for the target of bias.

words.example: Die Untersuchung war sehr umfangreich.
words.exampleTranslation: The investigation was very extensive.
words.whyItMatters: Often pairs with 'die Untersuchung' (investigation), 'die Recherche', 'die Sammlung', 'das Wissen'. From 'der Umfang' (extent/scope).

words.example: Die Kosten waren beträchtlich.
words.exampleTranslation: The costs were considerable.
words.whyItMatters: Formal alternative to 'erheblich' or 'groß'. Pairs with 'die Kosten', 'die Summe', 'der Aufwand', 'die Menge'. Journalistic register.

words.example: Seine Kritik ist gerechtfertigt.
words.exampleTranslation: His criticism is justified.
words.whyItMatters: Past participle of 'rechtfertigen' (to justify). Pairs with 'die Kritik', 'die Sorge', 'der Verdacht' (suspicion), 'die Entscheidung'.

words.example: Seine Analyse ist zu oberflächlich.
words.exampleTranslation: His analysis is too superficial.
words.whyItMatters: Compound: 'die Oberfläche' (surface) + '-lich'. Often pairs with 'die Analyse', 'das Wissen', 'der Eindruck'. Also describes shallow people.

words.example: Das Ergebnis ist eindeutig.
words.exampleTranslation: The result is unambiguous.
words.whyItMatters: Literally 'one-meaning'. Opposite 'zweideutig' (ambiguous/double-meaning). Pairs with 'das Ergebnis', 'die Antwort', 'der Beweis'.

words.example: Diese Technologie ist unverzichtbar geworden.
words.exampleTranslation: This technology has become indispensable.
words.whyItMatters: Compound: 'un-' + 'verzichten' (to do without). Often pairs with 'die Technologie', 'das Werkzeug', 'der Bestandteil' (component).

words.example: Die Unterschiede sind nur geringfügig.
words.exampleTranslation: The differences are only minor.
words.whyItMatters: From 'gering' (slight) + '-fügig'. Pairs with 'die Unterschiede', 'die Veränderung', 'die Verletzung'. Common in legal/medical contexts.

words.example: Seine Reaktion ist nachvollziehbar.
words.exampleTranslation: His reaction is understandable.
words.whyItMatters: Different from 'verständlich' (clear/easy to understand) — 'nachvollziehbar' specifically means 'I can follow the reasoning/feeling'. From 'nachvollziehen' (to trace, to follow).

words.example: Ziehe keine voreiligen Schlüsse!
words.exampleTranslation: Don't draw any hasty conclusions!
words.whyItMatters: Often pairs with 'die Schlüsse' (conclusions), 'die Entscheidung', 'das Urteil'. Set phrase: 'voreilige Schlüsse ziehen'.

words.example: Seine Aussage ist glaubwürdig.
words.exampleTranslation: His statement is credible.
words.whyItMatters: Compound: 'glauben' (believe) + '-würdig' (-worthy). Pairs with 'die Aussage', 'die Quelle' (source), 'der Zeuge' (witness). Journalistic/legal register.

words.example: Das ist ein anspruchsvoller Text.
words.exampleTranslation: That's a sophisticated text.
words.whyItMatters: Two uses: (1) demanding (task/job), (2) discerning (person with high standards). Pairs with 'die Aufgabe', 'der Job', 'das Publikum', 'der Geschmack'.

title: Trennbare Verben
description: Twelve sophisticated separable verbs — the verbs that appear in essays, news, and intellectual conversation.

words.example: Wir müssen uns an die neue Situation anpassen.
words.exampleTranslation: We have to adapt to the new situation.
words.whyItMatters: Reflexive AND separable: 'ich passe mich an'. Takes 'an + Akkusativ' for what you're adapting to. Perfekt: 'hat sich angepasst'.

words.example: Wir müssen herausfinden, was wirklich passiert ist.
words.exampleTranslation: We have to find out what really happened.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich finde das heraus'. Often followed by an indirect question (wer/was/wo/warum). Perfekt: 'hat herausgefunden'.

words.example: Es ist schwer, die Situation einzuschätzen.
words.exampleTranslation: It's hard to assess the situation.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich schätze das ein'. With 'zu' construction: 'einzuschätzen'. Perfekt: 'hat eingeschätzt'. Noun 'die Einschätzung' (assessment).

words.meaning: to engage with, to grapple with
words.example: Wir müssen uns mit dem Thema auseinandersetzen.
words.exampleTranslation: We have to engage with the topic.
words.whyItMatters: Reflexive AND separable. Splits: 'ich setze mich damit auseinander'. Takes 'mit + Dativ'. Perfekt: 'hat sich auseinandergesetzt'. Academic/intellectual register.

words.example: Ein Kompromiss ist endlich zustande gekommen.
words.exampleTranslation: A compromise has finally been reached.
words.whyItMatters: Fixed phrase — 'zustande' isn't a normal prefix. Splits: 'der Vertrag kommt zustande'. Takes 'sein' in Perfekt: 'ist zustande gekommen'. Formal/journalistic.

words.example: Ich schlage einen Kompromiss vor.
words.exampleTranslation: I'm proposing a compromise.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich schlage etwas vor'. Vowel change: 'du schlägst vor, er schlägt vor'. Noun 'der Vorschlag'. Perfekt: 'hat vorgeschlagen'.

words.example: Wir sollten alle Mitarbeiter in die Entscheidung einbeziehen.
words.exampleTranslation: We should involve all employees in the decision.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich beziehe ihn ein'. Takes 'in + Akkusativ' for what they're being included in. Perfekt: 'hat einbezogen' (Partizip II is irregular).

words.example: Welche Lösung sich langfristig durchsetzt, ist unklar.
words.exampleTranslation: Which solution will prevail long-term is unclear.
words.whyItMatters: Reflexive AND separable. Splits: 'er setzt sich durch'. Two uses: (1) a person asserting themselves, (2) an idea/solution winning out. Perfekt: 'hat sich durchgesetzt'.

words.meaning: to perform (in a test/competition); to cut off
words.example: Wie hast du in der Prüfung abgeschnitten?
words.exampleTranslation: How did you perform in the exam?
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'er schneidet gut ab'. Two meanings: (1) to perform/score (with adverb like 'gut/schlecht'), (2) literally to cut off. Perfekt: 'hat abgeschnitten'.

words.meaning: to fall back on, to resort to
words.example: In Notfällen greifen wir auf Ersparnisse zurück.
words.exampleTranslation: In emergencies we fall back on savings.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich greife darauf zurück'. Takes 'auf + Akkusativ'. Perfekt: 'hat zurückgegriffen'. Formal — common in essays about strategies/resources.

words.example: Viele nehmen das Problem nicht wahr.
words.exampleTranslation: Many people don't perceive the problem.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'ich nehme es wahr'. Vowel change: 'du nimmst wahr, er nimmt wahr'. Perfekt: 'hat wahrgenommen'. Noun 'die Wahrnehmung' (perception).

words.example: Das Treffen findet morgen statt.
words.exampleTranslation: The meeting takes place tomorrow.
words.whyItMatters: Splits: 'das Konzert findet statt'. Used only for events (no human subject). Perfekt: 'hat stattgefunden'. Essential for news/event vocabulary.
